planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; German Johnson is a large, pink-fleshed Southern heirloom widely grown across the Mid-Atlantic and Gulf Coast states for its outstanding flavor and generous yields of meaty, 1–2 pound fruits with a classic old-fashioned tomato taste.

directions: Start seeds indoors under grow lights 6–8 weeks before the last frost date; transplant outdoors after frost danger has fully passed with a wire cage or stout stake, setting the plant deep to encourage root formation along the buried stem. German Johnson is a large-fruited, indeterminate, pink-fleshed heirloom long popular throughout the American South and Mid-Atlantic states — it is considered a parent variety of the famous Mortgage Lifter, crossed by M.C. Byles of Logan, West Virginia to produce that legendary tomato; the fruits themselves are large, slightly irregular, meaty, and deeply flavored with a rich, slightly acidic, classic old-fashioned tomato taste and minimal gel content. The plants are vigorous and highly productive, with good resistance to common foliar diseases in hot, humid Southern conditions. Excellent for slicing, sandwiches, and home canning.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Sioux is one of the most heat-tolerant tomatoes available, originally developed by the University of Nebraska in the 1940s specifically for hot, humid Midwestern conditions and still treasured by Gulf Coast and Southern gardeners for its ability to set fruit through summer heat that defeats most other varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost and stake or cage early — Sioux is an indeterminate variety that sets fruit continuously through the season. Sioux was introduced by the University of Nebraska Agricultural Experiment Station in 1944, bred specifically for heat tolerance and disease resistance in hot, humid, continental climates; it remains one of the most reliably productive tomatoes for hot Southern summers and Gulf Coast conditions where other varieties drop blossoms during heat waves — Sioux continues setting medium-sized (6–8 oz), round, bright red fruits even when nighttime temperatures stay above 75°F. The flavor is classic, balanced, and mildly acidic — a dependable slicer with good disease resistance and continuous production from early summer until frost. Highly recommended for gardeners in zones 8–11 who struggle to keep tomatoes producing through midsummer.
sources: University of Nebraska Agricultural Experiment Station

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Marglobe was a landmark All-America Selections winner in 1925 when it became the first widely adopted disease-resistant tomato, and for decades it was the standard commercial and home garden tomato across the United States.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost danger passes and stake plants early. Marglobe, introduced by the USDA in 1925 and awarded the first All-America Selections Gold Medal, was the standard American home and market tomato for much of the twentieth century; bred at the Arlington, Virginia USDA experiment station to resist Fusarium wilt and nailhead rust, it was the first widely successful disease-resistant tomato and allowed commercial tomato production to expand dramatically into areas where disease pressure had previously made growing difficult. The fruits are medium-sized (6–8 oz), round to slightly globe-shaped, smooth, bright red, and of solid, crack-resistant construction with a firm skin and classic tomato flavor; Marglobe Supreme is a later improved selection. Grows as an indeterminate vine; productive over a long season and well-suited to home gardens, canning, and fresh market.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Big Beef is an All-America Selections winner hybrid that combines the large, meaty fruit of a beefsteak with broad disease resistance and consistent productivity — one of the most widely grown large-fruited tomatoes in American home gardens.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost and provide sturdy caging or staking for the vigorous indeterminate vines, which can reach 6 feet or more. Big Beef was introduced by Seminis Vegetable Seeds and won the All-America Selections award in 1994 — it has since become one of the best-selling tomato varieties in the United States, valued for combining the large size and exceptional flavor of old-fashioned beefsteak types with outstanding disease resistance (Verticillium and Fusarium wilt, nematodes, tobacco mosaic virus) that makes it more reliable across a wide range of climates and conditions. Fruits are large (8–12 oz or more), round to slightly oblate, meaty with few seeds and small blossom scars, and richly flavored — among the best-tasting large hybrid tomatoes available. Widely adaptable from cool northern gardens to Gulf Coast conditions.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Aunt Gertie's Gold is a large, golden-orange heirloom of Southern Appalachian origin with exceptionally sweet, low-acid, deeply flavored fruits that are among the finest of any golden tomato and particularly well-suited to hot, humid Southern summer conditions.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost has passed and provide staking or caging for the tall, indeterminate vines. Aunt Gertie's Gold is a Virginia heirloom reportedly traced to a Virginia woman named Aunt Gertie; it produces very large, golden-orange, oblate to slightly irregular fruits that can weigh 1–2 pounds, with sweet, rich, complex flavor that is noticeably lower in acid than red tomatoes — making it a preferred choice for people who find red tomatoes too acidic. The color is beautiful ranging from deep gold to orange-gold, and the fruits are meaty with dense flesh and few seeds. An outstanding fresh-eating tomato; the low-acid flesh is also excellent for sauces and drying. Grow as a standard indeterminate in full sun with consistent moisture and fertility.
